common_name: Florida Butterfly Orchid (Tampa Butterfly Orchid, Florida Encyclia)

planting.note: Mount on rough-barked wood or cork, or grow in a slatted basket with coarse bark; the Florida Butterfly Orchid grows naturally on live oak, bald cypress, buttonwood, and slash pine trees in southern Florida and performs best when its roots can cling to rough bark surfaces. Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun. Water regularly in active growth (spring–fall); reduce watering in winter dry rest. The fragrant flowers appear in summer. Wild plants are protected — only purchase from reputable nurseries that propagate legally. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow mounted on bark or in a slatted basket in bright indirect to filtered direct morning sun — Florida Butterfly Orchid grows most vigorously when its roots adhere to rough bark surfaces with excellent air circulation, mimicking its natural epiphytic habitat on Florida hardwood hammock trees. Encyclia tampensis is a small to medium epiphytic orchid native to Florida (Brevard County south through the Keys) and the Bahamas in the Orchidaceae family — the state wildflower of Florida, one of the most important and distinctive native orchids of the Sunshine State. The plant produces round to egg-shaped pseudobulbs with 2–3 narrow, leathery, dark-green leaves and, in summer, branched flower spikes bearing 5–45 small (1.5-inch) flowers. Each flower has narrow, greenish-bronze sepals and petals that frame a prominent, broad, white to pale lavender lip with a striking purple blotch or striping — the flower's color pattern of upright, narrow, insect-like dark-colored petals surrounding a prominent, pale, broad lip led to the common name 'Butterfly Orchid.' The flowers emit a pleasant, honey-like to spicy fragrance, strongest on warm mornings. Encyclia tampensis is the most frequently found native orchid in Florida's remaining hammock and cypress forests. Wild plants are protected by Florida law — all cultivated plants must derive from legally propagated nursery stock. An excellent native orchid for outdoor cultivation in zone 9b-12. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Encyclia tampensis: Florida's state wildflower orchid
Florida Native Plant Society — Encyclia tampensis species profile
American Orchid Society — Encyclia tampensis outdoor cultivation guide

common_name: Clamshell Orchid (Cockleshell Orchid, Black Orchid, Florida Native Clamshell)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork bark or in a slatted basket with coarse bark medium in bright indirect to partial direct morning sun; Prosthechea cochleata is a Florida native orchid that grows naturally on mangroves, buttonwood, and live oak trees in coastal hammocks. Water regularly in active growth; reduce in winter. This species blooms almost continuously in warm conditions — individual flower spikes open a succession of flowers over many months. Wild Florida plants are protected — purchase only nursery-propagated stock. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to partial direct morning light mounted on bark or in a slatted basket — Clamshell Orchid grows most vigorously with good air circulation and bright, filtered light similar to the dappled canopy light in its native Florida coastal hammock habitat. Prosthechea cochleata (formerly Epidendrum cochleatum) is an epiphytic orchid native to Florida (from Hillsborough and Volusia counties southward to the Keys), the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and northern South America in the Orchidaceae family, holding the distinction of being the national flower of Belize. The flower is immediately and unmistakably distinctive: unlike almost any other orchid, the flower is oriented upside down — the broadly shell-shaped (cochleate), dark purple to nearly black, deeply ribbed lip is at the TOP of the flower (not at the bottom as in most orchids), with the narrow, greenish-cream, twisted petals and sepals hanging downward below it like tentacles or a fringe. The visual impression is of a dark-purple clamshell or scallop shell with wispy tentacles — completely unlike any other common orchid flower. A further remarkable quality is that in warm conditions, Prosthechea cochleata blooms almost continuously — each spike continues opening new flowers over many months, making it one of the most persistent-blooming of any orchid species. A Florida native of distinctive character. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Prosthechea cochleata: Florida native clamshell orchid
Florida Native Plant Society — Prosthechea cochleata species profile
American Orchid Society — Prosthechea cochleata cultivation guide

common_name: Scarlet Ladies' Tresses (Leafless Beaked Orchid, Red Orchid Weed, Scarlet Sacoila)

planting.note: Grow in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, sandy to loamy, moderately fertile soil; Sacoila lanceolata is a leafless terrestrial orchid — it produces no green leaves, obtaining all nutrients through a mycorrhizal fungal relationship. Despite this, it can be grown successfully from bare-root tubers in sandy garden soil and will naturalize in disturbed, sunny ground in zone 8-12. Avoid heavy, clay-rich soils. Once established in appropriate sandy soil, it multiplies slowly and produces new stems each year. The bright scarlet-red flower spikes are among the showiest of any small native orchid. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, sandy, lean soil — Scarlet Ladies' Tresses is a uniquely leafless terrestrial orchid that grows in open, sunny, disturbed ground and performs best in lean, well-drained, sandy soil typical of Florida roadsides and sandhills. Sacoila lanceolata is a leafless, tuberous, mycoheterotrophic-assisted terrestrial orchid native to the southeastern United States (Florida, Georgia, South Carolina) and throughout the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and South America in the Orchidaceae family. The plant is completely leafless — it produces no photosynthetic foliage whatsoever, obtaining all its carbon from mycorrhizal fungal associates in a partial mycoheterotrophic relationship, supplemented by the chlorophyll that colors the green stem and flower parts. From the underground tubers, an erect, leafless flower spike emerges annually in spring and summer, ranging from 10–24 inches tall, bearing a dense spiral raceme of tubular, scarlet-red to hot coral-red flowers with slightly spreading, recurved petal tips. The intense, vivid red color of the flower spike is extraordinary for a native orchid and makes the plant visible from considerable distance in its native habitat of sunny Florida roadsides, flatwoods margins, and disturbed sandy ground. The species has a remarkable ability to appear and multiply in disturbed, sunny, sandy soil — it can colonize mowed roadsides and open garden beds, making it genuinely useful as a naturalizing native orchid for zone 8-12 landscapes. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Sacoila lanceolata: Scarlet Ladies' Tresses
Florida Native Plant Society — Sacoila lanceolata species profile

common_name: Lawn Orchid (Soldier's Orchid, Asian Lawn Orchid, Naturalized Jewel Orchid)

planting.note: Zeuxine strateumatica naturalizes spontaneously in lawns, turf, and disturbed ground throughout zone 8-12 and rarely needs to be intentionally planted — it appears on its own in maintained lawns and garden beds. If introducing deliberately, scatter seed in fall into moist, shaded to partially shaded lawn areas. In zone 9-12, the plant blooms in winter and early spring (November–March), then disappears as summer heat arrives. A delightful, harmless 'lawn weed' orchid that many Florida and Gulf Coast gardeners enjoy discovering for the first time in their St. Augustine or Bermuda grass lawns.

directions: Zeuxine strateumatica naturalizes spontaneously in lawns and turf throughout the southeastern US and requires no cultivation — it simply appears in suitable lawns and is a pleasant discovery rather than a planted species. Zeuxine strateumatica is a tiny, self-seeding, cool-season annual terrestrial orchid native to Southeast Asia and India in the Orchidaceae family — one of the most widely naturalized orchids in North America, now established throughout Florida, Hawaii, Louisiana, Texas, and other Gulf and Atlantic coastal states in maintained lawns, turf areas, and disturbed ground. In cool weather (November through March in zone 9-12), the plant produces small rosettes of narrow, grasslike, dark-green to bronze-green leaves that emerge from the lawn surface — often initially mistaken for a grass seedling or weed — and then an erect flower spike 4–8 inches tall bearing a raceme of tiny (0.25-inch) white to ivory flowers with a golden-yellow center, each flower perfect in miniature orchid form when viewed with a hand lens. The entire plant is small enough to be easily overlooked among lawn grass. Zeuxine strateumatica spreads prolifically by wind-dispersed dust seed and has colonized lawns throughout the southeastern US, the Caribbean, Central America, and Hawaii since its original naturalization in the early 20th century. Mowing is its only control — spring mowing eliminates the season's plants after they flower. A charming lawn companion orchid. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Zeuxine strateumatica: Florida's lawn orchid
Florida Native Plant Society — Zeuxine strateumatica naturalization notes
American Orchid Society — Zeuxine strateumatica: naturalized lawn orchid
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Naturalized Orchids of Florida

common_name: Monk Orchid (Spotted Orchid, African Spotted Orchid, Naturalized Shade Orchid)

planting.note: Oeceoclades maculata spreads spontaneously in shaded garden beds throughout zone 9-12 and is commonly encountered as a surprise arrival in mulched shade gardens rather than a deliberately planted species. If introducing deliberately, plant pseudobulbs in well-drained, humus-rich soil in shade under trees. The attractively spotted, succulent foliage is ornamental year-round. The plant produces white, purple-spotted flowers on erect spikes in summer. Has become naturalized and somewhat weedy in Florida and the Caribbean — check local invasive status before planting outside its native range.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in well-drained, humus-rich, dry to moderately moist soil — Monk Orchid is notably drought-tolerant for a terrestrial orchid and adapts well to the dry-shade conditions under mature trees where many other plants fail. Oeceoclades maculata is a small, drought-tolerant, terrestrial orchid native to tropical Africa, Madagascar, and Réunion in the Orchidaceae family, now widely naturalized throughout the Caribbean, Florida, Central America, and tropical South America. The plant produces clusters of small, oval, gray-green pseudobulbs with one or two succulent, leathery leaves: these leaves are the plant's main ornamental feature, patterned with irregular darker green mottling and spotting on a lighter gray-green background — a pattern similar to a jewel orchid and ornamental enough to make the plant attractive as a groundcover. From the pseudobulbs in summer, erect spikes bear 5–15 small flowers (0.5 inch) in white with purple speckling on the lip. Monk Orchid has naturalized widely in Florida and Caribbean shade gardens, often appearing spontaneously under live oaks, palms, and other shade trees in zone 9-12, spreading by wind-dispersed dust seed. It is considered a minor invasive in some Caribbean islands but is generally a harmless, even ornamental addition to shaded Florida and Gulf Coast gardens where its drought tolerance, attractive spotted foliage, and self-spreading habits make it useful as a low-maintenance shade groundcover. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Oeceoclades maculata: Monk Orchid in Florida
Florida Native Plant Society — Oeceoclades maculata naturalization notes
